name: ASTro AST-Based Reusable Optimisation Framework
summary: VMIL 2025: an AST-based reusable optimisation framework using AST semantics plus a partial-evaluator framework. Avoids semantic duplication between an interpreter and a compiler — the same AST representation drives both. Related to Truffle/Graal's partial-evaluation approach but reframed for reusability across languages.

sections.title: Overview
sections.content:
ASTro (VMIL 2025) is a research framework for reusable AST-based optimisation. The premise: the same AST representation that drives an interpreter can also drive a partial-evaluator-based compiler, eliminating the semantic duplication that arises when interpreter and compiler are written separately and must be kept in sync.

The framework provides AST semantics specifications plus a partial-evaluator infrastructure. A language implementer writes the AST and its semantics once; ASTro derives the JIT-compilable form via partial evaluation against the semantics specification.

sections.title: Design position
sections.content:
ASTro sits in the same design space as Truffle/Graal but with different emphasis. Truffle is tightly coupled to GraalVM's partial evaluator and to Java as the host language; ASTro frames the abstraction more reusably across languages and is positioned as a research framework rather than a production runtime.

The distinguishing claim is that the AST itself can be a reusable optimisation substrate — that the optimisation infrastructure can be written once and instantiated against multiple language ASTs without per-language compiler engineering. Whether this scales to production-grade performance is an open research question; the VMIL 2025 paper presents the framework as a starting point.
